experiments.title: heuristic tool selector (keyword-based top-3)
experiments.description:
# experiment: heuristic tool selector (keyword-based top-3)

## Hypothesis

Adding a deterministic, lightweight keyword-based tool selector that restricts the agent to the top-3 most relevant tools per task will (1) increase reliably passing cases by reducing tool confusion/noise and (2) reduce average passing-case time by avoiding unnecessary tool invocations. This targets both levers: increase passing cases and reduce mean passing-case time.

## Motivation

The repo's ideas emphasize a small harness and tool-selection as high-impact, low-cost changes. A full ML-based selector is heavy; a simple keyword-overlap heuristic is cheap to implement (<50 LOC), deterministic, and likely to avoid worst-case tool misuses that harm correctness and latency.

## Type

Prompting / tooling experiment (agent prompt + lightweight helper)

## Mutation surface

- agents/edge.py (single-file change)

## Implementation Instructions

1. Add a small helper near prompt-build logic in agents/edge.py (<= ~40 LOC):
   - def select_tools_by_keywords(prompt: str, tools: list[dict], k: int = 3) -> list[dict]:
       - Tokenize prompt and each tool's name/short description (split on non-word chars, lowercased).
       - Score each tool by keyword overlap count (intersection size).
       - Tie-break deterministically by tool name.
       - Return top-k tools (or all tools if all scores are zero).
2. Integrate the helper into the prompt construction path:
   - Before serializing available tools into the agent prompt, call select_tools_by_keywords(user_prompt, tools, k=3).
   - Only include the returned tools' descriptions in the prompt (keep the same format as existing tool docs so downstream parsing is unchanged).
3. Add a small config constant near the top of agents/edge.py: TOOL_SELECTOR_K = 3 to make tuning easy.
4. Keep behaviour non-destructive: when debug mode or an env var (EDGE_AGENT_TOOL_SELECTOR=off) is set, fall back to including all tools.

Notes on implementation style:
- Keep changes minimal and local to agents/edge.py (no new deps).
- Use plain Python sets and sorted() for deterministic ties.
- Add unit-friendly separation: keep select_tools_by_keywords as a pure function so tests can call it.

## Validation(code, prompts, tasks)

- Run the agent smoke prompts before/after the change:
  - just edge-agent "What is the age the 3rd president of the US when he took office?"
  - just edge-agent "Summarize the last 24 hours system logs and flag anomalies"
- Run the evals runner for the edge agent baseline set and compare metrics:
  - just eval "edge_agent_default"  (compare passing_cases and avg_passing_case_seconds against baseline)
- Inspect traces/logs to confirm:
  - Only up to 3 tools are included in the prompt per task.
  - Tool invocation counts decrease or stay same; correctness does not regress.

Success criteria:
- No regressions in passing cases (ideally +1 or more)
- Average passing-case seconds reduced by >=5% on passing cases

## Anticipated missteps and fallbacks

- Selector returns zero overlap for many tasks, causing fallback to all tools — acceptable; if it happens often, change k to 5 or augment scoring with simple tf-idf weights.
- Selector omits a crucial but low-overlap tool (e.g., diagnostics tool invoked by generic prompts). Fallback: expose EDGE_AGENT_TOOL_SELECTOR_K env var and allow per-task overrides in the prompt metadata.
- If deterministic ties cause pathological ordering, add a short priority map for essential tools (configurable list) to ensure critical tools are retained.

## Sources

- /workspace/docs/ideas.md — suggested Harness + Model Routing and tool-selection follow-up
- LangChain agent architecture posts (conceptual guidance on tool selectors)

## Notes

- Mutation is intentionally small (<=50 LOC) and dependency-free to fit repo constraints.
- This experiment targets both reliability and latency; if successful, follow-ups can explore lightweight ML-based selectors or usage-based caching.
"
